story:
Every spring, on the first Monday of April, Congress gathered to renew the laws of physics. It had become a quick ritual: the House and Senate sped through Newton’s three laws and Einstein’s relativity, stamped them into place, and sent the President to the Rose Garden to sign reality into another year of service. Headlines yawned, “Routine Reaffirmation of Reality Successful,” and nobody worried. Inside, though, the ritual carried weight. The Sergeant at Arms held the gavel like a relic. A clerk carried in a glass pendulum that swung with solemn purpose beneath humming fluorescent lights. Members wore their allegiance openly—apple pins for Newtonians, Einstein’s grinning face for Einsteinians—and the vote usually ended before lunch. Gravity never got any lighter.

This year, voters split the chambers nearly down the middle. The old calm snapped into sharp argument. Congressman Crane of Vermont waved off Newton as antique math, shouting that modern science could do better. Representative Mallory clutched her apple pin and snapped back that ignoring gravity would send oceans and children into the sky. The Speaker, tired and rumpled, banged the gavel. He did not finish his warning. He didn’t need to. The universe seemed to hold its breath.

What had always been ceremonial turned into a brawl with microphones. Members proposed amendments to the laws and filibustered with dull endurance. Someone suggested tabling the discussion for civility’s sake and was accused of treachery. The clerk blinked awake and watched the pendulum’s steady arc, even as tempers frayed. Outside, crowds mimicked their leaders. Newtonians threw apples; Einsteinians threw textbooks. Signs bobbed—“Support Newton!” and “Embrace Relativity!”—and a lone man stood in between with a placard that read, “Reality Is Independent of Politics.”

By afternoon, coffee cups carpeted the floors. Members sagged in their seats, hungry, cranky, and still unyielding. The Speaker straightened his papers. “First motion,” he called, “to reaffirm the validity of Einstein’s General Theory of Relativity.” Roll call proceeded. Einsteinians answered, “Aye”; Newtonians fired back, “Nay.” The motion failed. Silence pooled. Then came the Newtonian motion. “Aye,” shouted apples; “Nay,” replied tongues. It failed too. Papers shuffled. “Both motions have failed. The House is adjourned,” the Speaker said, and he walked out.

Panic rushed in to fill the silence. Lawmakers grabbed desks and hurled blame across the aisle. “Hold on to something!” Mallory cried. The pendulum kept swinging. Lights hummed. Knuckles whitened; parents held their children; a man at a bar guessed there might be a delay before reality noticed.

Nothing happened. The world stayed put. Minutes marched by. Crane stood, baffled. “That’s it?” A Newtonian’s voice cracked: “We’ve fought over nothing?” The Speaker returned, calmer, his tie fixed. Above him, painted philosophers and gods stared down without comment. Members slipped away, avoiding reporters’ questions and insults.

The Sergeant at Arms collected the gavel and papers. He met the Speaker’s tired eyes and said softly, “See you next April.”

By midnight, the country understood the joke had been on them. The grand ritual had never held the cosmos together. Politicians resigned; parties unraveled; voters demanded something real. Asked how to cure a self-inflicted wound, the President could only say, “I don’t know how to fix this. Our nation is forever scarred.”

Overhead, the stars continued their patient work—burning, collapsing, drifting—without signatures, without caucuses, without applause. No approval was needed.
